main_sections.title: Carrier of Words
main_sections.prose_summary: The prose narrative centres on Khetaram, a Gramin Dak Sewak (GDS) posted at the Somarad Branch Post Office in the Thar desert of Rajasthan, just 2.5 km from the Indo-Pakistan border. The piece opens by contrasting the ease of today's digital communication with the arduous reality of postal delivery in India's remotest corners — beyond the last railhead, beyond the last phone line, and where roads dissolve into sand too soft for bicycles. Khetaram, whose left shoulder has visibly slumped from fifteen years of carrying a mailbag weighing up to 28 kilos, is the sole link between scattered desert hamlets (dhaanis) and the outside world. He covers up to 20 km for a single delivery, delivering even when temperatures unofficially cross 50 degrees Celsius, walking in his mojri-clad feet across scorching sand beds and dunes. On other days, sandstorms coat him in sand until he resembles a walking sandman, and water is too precious to waste on washing. The narrative contextualises Khetaram's role within the broader GDS system: until 2001 these workers were called 'delivery agents'; today India's three-lakh-plus GDS workforce constitutes over half of India Post's total workforce, serving the frozen deserts of Ladakh, the islands of Lakshadweep, and the riverine communities of the northeast. Unlike the British postal system, which was set up to relay company dak between administrative centres, post-independence India Post was mandated to bring the entire population within the mailing ambit — growing from 25,000 post offices in 1947 to over 1.5 lakh today. For Khetaram personally, the job is existential: in a good year he harvests one crop of bajra, which cannot feed his family of five; without the salary, they would starve. The money order, as Mulk Raj Anand noted in Story of the Indian Post Office, is uniquely central to Indian rural life, and villages fought off a proposal to replace GDS with patwaris, trusting only the familiar postman. Khetaram is welcomed on every threshold — he reads letters aloud and drafts replies in his shaky hand. He receives small gestures of warmth: a cup of tea from the BSF camp, a piece of jaggery on news of births or weddings. The one delivery he dreads is the envelope with its right corner torn off — the sign of a death notice (Ashubh Samachar) — which he reads aloud twice outside the house and then tears to bits, believing bad news must be destroyed. The piece closes with Khetaram waiting optimistically for phone lines to reach his territory, when he hopes to become a Gramin Sanchar Sewak, carrying both a cell phone and the post. His words — 'Main tayyar hoon' (I'm ready) — capture his enduring spirit. The author pays tribute to people like Khetaram as integral threads in India's social fabric.

reading_for_comprehension.section_name: Reflect and Respond + Reading for Meaning + Check Your Understanding + Critical Reflection

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Look at the pictures given below of persons engaged in the same profession. Discuss in pairs and share your answers with your classmates and teacher.

question_bank.stem: Which profession do they belong to?
Do they face any difficulties while doing their duty?
How does their profession affect the people they meet?

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Discuss in groups.

question_bank.stem: How can 'words' be 'carried'?
Who could be a carrier of words?
What are the means for people to stay connected?

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Read the words given in the box below (slumped, dunes, defying, hamlets, signals, devoid) and place them in their appropriate context in the following passage.

question_bank.stem: our body sent 1. _________ that our decision to visit the sand 2. _________ in the afternoon was insane
We had passed a few 3. _________ on our way to the desert
We could not find even a single soul outdoors, 4. _________ the blazing sun
Even the camels were 5. _________ in the little shade offered by the khejri trees
the area was 6. _________ of any other network service

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Khetaram needs to submit information about himself for getting an identity card. While reading the text, fill up the following information sheet for Khetaram.

question_bank.stem: Fill information sheet: Position, Employer, Official address, Mode of transport used, Key responsibilities (4 points)

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Discuss with your teacher.

question_bank.stem: Why do you think Khetaram has taken up this challenging job?
How do you think Khetaram would feel about taking up this challenging task every day?

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Complete the table given below with the situations given in Column 1 and the reasons in Column 2.

question_bank.stem: Situation for reason: 'When the temperature is recorded as 50 degrees celsius and above, a state holiday has to be declared.'
Reason for situation: 'Khetaram can dwell on any threshold.'
Reason for situation: 'BSF always gave Khetaram a lift.'

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Why was a piece of jaggery offered in the village when he brought news of a birth or weddings?
How would phone lines help Khetaram in his job?

Read the extracts given below and answer the questions. Extract 1: "Akaal or famine is a way of life here. In a good year, I get one crop of bajra. That cannot feed my family of five. We would starve without this job," he says. And for each family which resides in this arid land, survival is partly dependent on money orders remitted by a relative.

question_bank.stem: Complete the sentence: One crop of bajra makes a little difference for people like Khetaram because __________.
Why did Khetaram term famine as 'a way of life'?
Give one reason why Khetaram had to take up a job to help his family to survive.
Complete the analogy: Other families: Money orders :: Khetaram: __________
What would 'a good year' signify?

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Read the extracts given below and answer the questions. Extract 2: There is one letter delivery he dreads. The envelope with the right corner torn off, which signifies that the missive bears news of death. "Ashubh Samachar cannot be carried into the house," says Khetaram. So, he stands outside, reads out the letter twice, then tears it to bits. "Bad news must be destroyed," he mutters philosophically.

question_bank.stem: Who could have torn the right corner of the envelope? A. Khetaram  B. Sender of the letter  C. Receiver of the letter  D. The postmaster
Complete the sentence with an appropriate reason: The most likely reason for Khetaram reading the letter twice is because __________.
What was Khetaram's philosophy regarding bad news, and how did he act upon it?
State whether true or false: Khetaram's action of tearing the letter differed from his beliefs.
Mention any one aspect of Khetaram's temperament which is revealed through this extract.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Answer the following questions.

question_bank.stem: Justify the statement made by Postmaster-General, Rajasthan Western Region stating that the role of GDS is 'invaluable'.
How was the purpose of India Post different from that of the British postal system?
People trusted Gramin Dak Sewaks. Support this statement with any two instances from the text.
Infer the most likely reason that Khetaram was grateful to continue as GDS, even after the age of 60.
Explain why the author pays tribute to people like Khetaram.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Study the phrases given in Column 1 (crumbles into sand; give a new lease of life; turn into a trickle before drying out; bearing words across this desolate geography) and match them with their meanings in Column 2. Now, use the phrases from Column 1 in sentences of your own.

question_bank.stem: crumbles into sand — match meaning
give a new lease of life — match meaning
turn into a trickle before drying out — match meaning
bearing words across this desolate geography — match meaning

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Identify examples of alliteration and metaphor in the extract: 'Khetaram's khaki turban and uniform are his only protection against the desert's furies, the scorching summer winds and swirling sandstorms which turn him into a walking sandman.'
Match the words in Column 1 (arid; concessional; gaunt; remote; desolate) to what they suggest in Column 2 in the context of the chapter. Now, use the words from Column 1 in sentences of your own.

question_bank.stem: arid — match contextual meaning
concessional — match contextual meaning
gaunt — match contextual meaning
remote — match contextual meaning
desolate — match contextual meaning

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Read the underlined verbs in Column 1 and their functions in Column 2 (present perfect tense: has/have + past participle). Now, fill in the blanks by using the present perfect form of the verbs given in brackets to complete the following paragraph about postage stamps.

question_bank.stem: I _________ (has/have) just ________ (get) a collection of postage stamps from my grandfather.
He 2. ___________ (collect) these stamps over a period of 20 years.
I 3. ___________ (study) most of the stamps in his collection.
I 4. _______ (begin) my own collection of postage stamps.
Grandfather 5. _________ truly ________ (inspire) me.

Match the 7 sentences in Column 1 to their present perfect tense functions in Column 2 (finished actions, recently completed, life experiences, accomplishment, changes over time, multiple actions, action past with present impact)

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Study the sentences (active vs passive voice) and complete the following paragraph about advice given by Khetaram to his nephew by converting active sentences into passive voice. Fill blanks A–E.

question_bank.stem: Next, reading clearly A. ________________________, as you B. ________________________ to read out the letters by some of the villagers.
Words C. ________________________ neatly as you D. ________________________ to draft replies by some villagers.
Full sleeved-shirts and trousers E. ________________________ to protect yourself from the heat.

listening.transcript_text: Speaker: Bhavna, Grade 9B. Good morning. She announces the celebration of Indian Postal Day on 10 October to commemorate India Post, described as the most widely functioning government postal service in the world. The school will organise 'Daakroom' — a letter-writing carnival. The name comes from the Hindi word 'daak' meaning 'post'. The objective is to revive letter writing as a forgotten art form. The carnival will feature competitions, events, and workshops including letter writing, stamp collecting, calligraphy, origami, graphology (study of handwriting), and postcard making. It will also include music, theatre, and dance, as well as interactive demonstrations by the postal department. Bhavna concludes by requesting all students to actively participate to make the event a grand success.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: You will listen to a girl talking about an event. As you listen, fill in the blank with the suitable word from the brackets. The speaker concluded her talk with a ___________. (suggestion/request/advice)

question_bank.stem: The speaker concluded her talk with a ___________. (suggestion/request/advice)

tasks_verbatim.task_text: You will listen to the girl talking about the event again. As you listen, choose the correct option for the following questions.

question_bank.stem: What was the occasion that the speaker was talking about? (i) Letter writing carnival  (ii) Philately day  (iii) Indian Postal Day
India Post is famous for having the most extensive network _________. (i) in India  (ii) in the world  (iii) among design institutes
Letter writing is being revived as this art form has been _________. (i) celebrated  (ii) appreciated  (iii) forgotten
Which of the following will be a part of the letter writing carnival? (i) calligraphy and graphology  (ii) origami and designing  (iii) letter posting and philately
What will the postal department add to the event? (i) interactive demonstrations  (ii) theatre and dance  (iii) workshop for letter writing

tasks_verbatim.task_text: The postcard, inland letter, envelope, and money order form were engaged in a discussion on who the most important one was. In groups of four, select one each and reason with each other logically. You may use the points for discussion (utility, cost effective, privacy, space to write, possibility of enclosures) and the verbal cues in Column 2 (It seems to me that...; Some people say that...; I would like to point out that...; I totally agree that...; That's one way to look at it but...) to express your opinion.

A condolence message communicates your feelings to someone whose relative or a person close to them has just passed away. Khetaram saw a letter with a torn corner. It had news about Bhawar Singh's uncle passing away. Read the condolence message that Sawant Singh sent to his cousin as a reply to the letter. Now, on behalf of your parents draft an imaginary condolence message for your aunt on the recent passing away of her father.

The unique 'Daakroom' carnival was conceptualised by the Ministry of Culture to re-introduce children and larger audiences to letter writing in innovative, creative, and engaging ways. Read about the carnival (supported by India Post, Ministry of Culture, Gandhi Smriti and Darshan Samiti) aimed to give a digital detox and revive the art of letter writing in India. (Source: https://www.pib.gov.in/PressReleasePage.aspx?PRID=1880828)

Did you know? The study of postage stamps and postal history is called philately. It is possible to be a philatelist without owning any stamps. The stamps that you study may be rare ones and found only in museums. Study the postage stamps given and discuss who or what they depict. Work in groups of five to find information about stamps on the following categories: personalities (name and field), celebrations (festivals/national events), centenaries, nature, our heritage (monuments/music/dance). Prepare a class board on 'Philately–Upholding Our Heritage' with your findings.

In the past, telegram was a mode of fast communication, commonly used to quickly send short and specific messages, such as greetings and condolences. Its pricing structure was based on the number of words to be transmitted, and therefore, needed to be precise. Study the inland telegram form given. Use this knowledge to compose your own telegram message for an appropriate occasion.
